title: Magnetism and Topology from Circularly Polarized Phonon Floquet Engineering
authors: Dapeng Yao, Tiantian Zhang, Takashi Oka, Takehito Yokoyama
abstract: We theoretically show that circularly polarized phonons induce electronic magnetization and drive a topological phase transition via phonon Floquet engineering. Considering the electronic states modulated by circularly polarized phonons on a honeycomb lattice, we show that such lattice dynamics generates an effective next-nearest-neighbor electron hopping, leading to a Haldane-type mass term. Circularly polarized phonon breaks time-reversal symmetry (TRS) and opens a gap at valley points, undergoing phase transition from a trivial insulator to a Chern insulator. Moreover, the orbital and spin magnetizations emerge due to the breaking of TRS. Our results show that circularly polarized phonons serve as an effective magnetic field to engineer magnetism and topology, offering new opportunities for phonon Floquet approaches.
summary: This theoretical paper shows that coupling electrons on a honeycomb lattice to circularly polarized phonons can act as an effective magnetic field. By employing Floquet engineering, the system undergoes a controllable topological phase transition into a Chern insulator. This mechanism not only generates non-zero orbital and spin magnetizations but also provides a novel pathway for realizing topological materials using purely vibrational driving.
main_problem: The study demonstrates how circularly polarized phonons can induce electronic magnetization and drive a topological phase transition in a honeycomb lattice via phonon Floquet engineering.
main_result: Circularly polarized phonons act as an effective magnetic field, inducing a tunable topological phase transition from a trivial insulator to a Chern insulator, while also generating measurable orbital and spin magnetizations.
method: The authors utilize Floquet theory in the high-frequency limit ($\Omega^{-1}$) to derive an effective Hamiltonian that incorporates phonon-mediated hopping terms, allowing for gap analysis at valley points.
model_or_system: The model consists of electrons on a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice coupled to time-periodic driving from circularly polarized phonons. The physics is analyzed using the derived Floquet effective Hamiltonian, which generates an effective next-nearest-neighbor hopping term.
key_observables: Chern number ($C$), orbital magnetization ($M_z$), and spin magnetization ($\langle S_z angle$).
important_parameters: Phonon frequency ($\Omega$), lattice parameters ($t_0, \delta$), and the strength of Rashba SOC ($\lambda_R$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a van-Vleck type expansion in the inverse phonon frequency ($\Omega^{-1}$) for the effective Hamiltonian derivation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate band structures showing gap opening due to phonons, phase diagrams mapping the Chern number dependence on SOC strength and phonon amplitude, and schematics of the induced NNN hopping term.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the initial electron-phonon coupled Hamiltonian to deriving the high-frequency effective Floquet Hamiltonian. It then analyzes the resulting band structure near valley points to calculate topological invariants (Chern number) and magnetizations, concluding with a discussion of how phonon pumping controls these signs.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it uses time-periodic driving (Floquet engineering) in condensed matter systems—a core topic in AMO physics—to engineer fundamental quantum properties like topology and magnetism using lattice vibrations rather than external magnetic fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the phonon-driven electron systems on a two-dimensional honeycomb lattice, where a circularly polar- ized phonon in optical branches at the Brillouin-zone center is excited. Electron-phonon coupling enables phonon-assisted nearest-neighbor hopping via phonon emission and absorption processes, which generates an effective next-nearest-neighbor hopping. This process drives the system to a topological phase with a nonzero Chern number. Spin and orbital magnetiza- tions emerge due to the breaking of time-reversal symmetry by circularly polarized phonons.

FIG. 2. Topological phase transition driven by circularly po- larized phonons. (a) Band structures of the spinless effective Hamiltonian heff(k) along the high-symmetry points with and without phonon dynamics. We set the on-site potential δ = 0, and the phonon rotational amplitude ur = 0.1a0, respectively. Inset shows the bands near the K point. (b) Phase diagram of the Chern number as a function of ur/a0 and δ/t0. (c) Band structure of the spinful Hamiltonian Heff(k) with the on-site potential δ = 0.005t0, Rashba SOC λR = 0.4t0, and the phonon rotational amplitude ur/a0 = 5%. Inset shows the bands around the valley points. (d) Phase diagram of the Chern number as a function of ur/a0 and...

FIG. 3. Orbital magnetization driven by circularly polarized phonons. (a-1) and (a-2) Intrinsic magnetic moment distri- bution for the valence band along the high-symmetry points. (b-1) and (b-2) Berry curvature distribution for the valence band along the high-symmetry points. The results without phonon dynamics are shown in (a-1) and (b-1), and those with phonon dynamics for different Chern numbers are shown in (a-2) and (b-2). Red and blue lines in (a-2) and (b-2) rep- resent ur/a0 = 6% (C = 0) and ur/a0 = 10% (C = −1), respectively. (c-1) and (c-2) Chemical potential dependence of the z component of the orbital magnetization. (c-2) en- larges the region around the band gap. Phonon energy...

FIG. 4. Spin magnetization driven by circularly polarized phonons. (a) and (b) Chemical potential dependence of the z-component spin expectation value with the colors repre- senting the Rashba SOC and phonon rotational amplitude, respectively. The on-site potential is set to be δ = 0.02t0. We fix the phonon rotational amplitude ur/a0 = 10% in (a) and the Rashba SOC λR = 0.4t0 in (b). (c) Band structure with the on-site potential δ = 0.02t0, the phonon rotational am- plitude ur/a0 = 8%, and the Rashba SOC λR = 0.4t0. The color represents the spin texture ⟨ˆSz⟩nk/(ℏ/2) of each band at k point. Phonon energy and temperature are set to be ℏΩ= 0.2t0 and kBT = 0.03t0, respectively.
